english: Chinese fried rice with shrimp and BBQ pork

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no Yangzhou-specific entry exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers fried rice as a category; no Yangzhou-specific code exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Yangzhou Fried Rice or generic fried rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Yangzhou Fried Rice is approximately 300 g (one plate or bowl), providing roughly 525 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fried rice with meat and vegetables (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for Yangzhou Fried Rice exists. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central composite entries for fried rice with egg, shrimp, and pork (SR Legacy), cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo data for Chinese fried rice dishes. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) were reviewed against these approved sources and found to be within plausible ranges; values retained with adaptation notation. Saturated fat estimated at 1.8 g/100 g based on oil and pork fat contribution consistent with USDA fried rice composites.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Yangzhou Fried Rice found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for mixed fried rice with protein and fat components (white rice GI ~72 attenuated by egg, shrimp, pork, and oil content). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 67.5) / 100 = 43.9 g per 300 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (310 mg/100 g; ~930 mg per 300 g serving) from soy sauce and char siew
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request reduced soy sauce, and avoid adding extra salt or condiments to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2,000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate-to-high glycemic load (GL ~43.9 per 300 g serving) from refined white rice, partially attenuated by protein and fat co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict serving size to 150–200 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycemic response, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the medium GI of 65 and substantial carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (310 mg/100 g) and moderate fat content (6.5 g/100 g) including saturated fat from char siew and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request preparation with less oil and reduced char siew, and balance the meal with fibre-rich vegetables to mitigate the sodium and saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yangzhou Fried Rice (扬州炒饭 / 揚州炒飯; pinyin: Yángzhōu chǎofàn) is one of the most internationally recognised varieties of Chinese fried rice, originating from Yangzhou city in Jiangsu province, China. The dish is prepared by wok-frying day-old steamed white rice over high heat with beaten eggs, shelled shrimp, diced char siew (Chinese BBQ pork), scallions, green peas, and diced carrots, seasoned with soy sauce, salt, and a small amount of sesame oil. The hallmark technique is 'gold covered with silver' (金包银), in which each grain of rice is individually coated with egg before the other ingredients are folded in, producing a fluffy, non-clumping texture with distinct grains. The flavour profile is savoury and mildly smoky from the wok hei (breath of the wok), with contrasting textures from the tender shrimp, chewy pork, and soft egg. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 525 kcal, 21.6 g protein, 19.5 g fat, and 67.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is notable at ~930 mg per serving, primarily from soy sauce and char siew. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~65) due to the moderating effect of protein and fat on the glycaemic response of white rice. It is a complete one-dish meal suitable for lunch or dinner but should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, carbohydrate, and fat content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Yangzhou fried rice?
What is the difference between Yangzhou fried rice and regular fried rice?
Is Yang Chow fried rice the same as Yangzhou fried rice?
Can diabetics eat Yangzhou fried rice?
How much sodium is in Yangzhou fried rice?
What are the ingredients in 扬州炒饭?

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry for Yangzhou Fried Rice exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried rice composites and cross-referenced with HPB Chinese fried rice data. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) were found consistent with these references and retained. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for Yangzhou Fried Rice as a composite dish are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried rice entries or Singapore HPB Foodo as of April 2026; these fields are therefore unavailable from any approved source consulted. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central for comparable fried rice composites; no significant vitamin D source is present in the standard recipe. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA fried rice composite data reflecting typical vegetable oil and char siew fat contribution. The input portions array contained a duplicate entry (two identical 300 g default portions); deduplicated to a single 300 g default serving. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for Yangzhou Fried Rice has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Halal status is marked false because char siew (Chinese BBQ pork) is a pork product; a halal variant substituting chicken or beef char siew would change this flag. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact Yangzhou-specific codes exist in either system.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is the precise canonical English name 'Yangzhou Fried Rice'. Chinese simplified and traditional forms present. Cuisine is Huaiyang/Jiangsu; G6 does not mandate Malay/Tamil/Hindi for this non-Singaporean dish.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (175.0), protein_g (7.2), fat_g (6.5), carbohydrate_g (22.5) all present and numeric. nutrient_source cites USDA FoodData Central with adaptation notes. No core macro is zero.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.8), sodium_mg (310.0), saturated_fat_g (1.8), sugar_g (0.4) all present as numeric values and plausible.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (65), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (43.9), and gi_source all present. GI sourced from peer-reviewed journal (AJCN 2021 international GI tables) as analogous estimate with documented basis.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn closest_parent match FOODON_00002694 (fried rice food product) and FoodEx2 closest_parent match A0CKW (fried rice) applied with gap notes. Match types corrected to 'closest_parent' which is an allowed value per the validator schema ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. No codes invented. SNOMED CT null with gap note.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,350 characters), covers preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Human condition name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains all strings from aliases_by_language (verified: 'Yangzhou Fried Rice', 'Yang Chow Fried Rice', 'Yeung Chow Fried Rice', 'Chinese fried rice with shrimp and BBQ pork', '扬州炒饭', '揚州炒飯', 'Yángzhōu chǎofàn', 'joeng4 zau1 caau2 faan6'). Alternate English names, Chinese script forms, pinyin, and Cantonese romanization all captured.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Documented all -1 sentinel fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, water_g) in the 'notes' field to address g3_numeric_hygiene failure from iteration 1.

Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broad' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enum values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by g5_ontology_honesty. This resolves the g5 hard gate failure reported by the validator in iteration 2.
Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to explicitly state that -1 fields are unavailable from any approved source consulted, strengthening g3 compliance documentation.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Yangzhou Fried Rice (EX000126) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8. The iteration-2 REJECT was caused by (1) g3: -1 sentinel fields not documented in notes, and (2) g5: ontology match types set to 'broad' which is not in the allowed enum ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. Both issues are resolved in this iteration: all -1 fields are fully documented in notes with source consultation context, and ontology match types are corrected to 'closest_parent'. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried rice composites with HPB cross-reference. GI estimated from AJCN 2021 peer-reviewed international GI tables. Ontology anchors are honest closest-parent matches with gap notes. All three mandatory health conditions present with clinical specificity. Multilingual coverage includes Chinese simplified and traditional; G6 does not require Malay/Tamil/Hindi for a Jiangsu Chinese dish. Alias consistency verified across aliases and aliases_by_language. Record is LLM-ready.
